text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments, including monopolar scissors, to open the gastrocolic window, and a bipolar vessel sealing device is demonstrated. The procedure is performed entirely from the console by a single surgeon.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to mobilize the greater curvature of the stomach, detaching posterior attachments and taking down the hepatic flexure of the colon. Epiploic vessels are identified and divided using locking plastic clips, including the right gastric artery. The duodenum is identified, mobilized, stapled, and divided with a robotic stapling device, followed by completing its mobilization with an extensive Coker maneuver.

The surgeon creates a window in the greater omentum to access the proximal jejunum, stapling it approximately twenty centimeters distal to the ligament of Treitz. The mesentery of the proximal jejunum is secured using a vessel sealing device. Attention is then directed to the portohepatic region, where the gastroduodenal artery is identified, ligated with silk sutures, locked with plastic clips, and divided, revealing the anterior aspect of the portal vein posterior to the common hepatic artery.

The surgeon controls branches off the superior mesenteric vein with locking clips and creates a tunnel underneath the neck of the pancreas. The neck of the pancreas is suspended using umbilical tape and divided with monopolar scissors. Attention is then turned to the uncinate process, where the superior mesenteric artery is identified and dissected using a robotic bipolar vessel sealing device, with occasional use of clips for the inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery.

The surgeon divides the cystic duct and cystic artery, preparing the gallbladder for removal as a separate specimen. The common hepatic duct is also divided. The biliary pancreatic limb is brought up through the transverse colon mesentery to reach the right upper quadrant for reconstruction.

The surgeon performs a pancreaticojejunostomy using a running absorbable monofilament suture on the posterior pancreatic wall. The patient's pancreatic parenchyma appears atrophic, with a large pancreatic duct measuring approximately five to six millimeters in diameter. A ductomucosal anastomosis is created with six-oh monofilament absorbable sutures, placing six or seven sutures. A seven French stent is inserted into the pancreatic duct, although its necessity is questioned due to the duct's size.

The surgeon completes the closure of the anterior pancreatic duct wall using a running 4-0 monofilament absorbable suture. A hepatico-jejunostomy is performed using a 4-0 monofilament absorbable barbed suture, starting with the posterior wall and then the anterior wall in a circumferential running fashion. The round ligament vascularized pedicled flap is placed posterior to the pancreatic autogenostomy. The jejunum is brought up in an anticolic fashion and suspended to the posterior gastric antrum with a running absorbable monofilament suture to relieve tension on the anastomosis.

The surgeon opens the staple line on the proximal duodenum and creates a matching enterotomy in the jejunum. The posterior wall is sutured using a running absorbable monofilament barbed suture, known as a running baseball stitch, which joins all layers of both bowel walls. The remaining staple line from the duodenum is removed, and the anterior wall is closed with a four-oh monofilament absorbable suture, inverting the duodenum and jejunum to prevent protruding mucosa, using a running limbert stitch.
And this completes our single surgeon robotic Whipple.
